# FluidVoice

[](#fluidvoice)

[![Supported Models](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://huggingface.co/nvidia/parakeet_realtime_eou_120m-v1)

Fully open source voice-to-text dictation app for macOS with AI enhancement.

**Install with Homebrew:** `brew install --cask fluidvoice`

**Manual download:** [latest release](https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice/releases/latest)

Important

This project is completely free and open source. If you find FluidVoice useful, please star the repository. It helps with visibility and motivates continued development. Your support means a lot.

## Latest Update

[](#latest-update)

-   Added **[Parakeet Flash (Beta)](https://huggingface.co/nvidia/parakeet_realtime_eou_120m-v1)** for low-latency live English dictation on Apple Silicon
-   Added **Cohere Transcribe** support for higher-accuracy multilingual dictation on Apple Silicon
-   Expanded the voice engine lineup with **Parakeet Flash, Parakeet v3/v2, Cohere, Apple Speech, and Whisper**

## Features

[](#features)

-   **Live Preview Mode**: Real-time transcription preview in overlay
-   **Multiple Speech Models**: Parakeet Flash, Parakeet TDT v3 & v2, Cohere Transcribe, Apple Speech, and Whisper
-   **Real-time transcription** with extremely low latency
-   **AI enhancement** with OpenAI, Groq, and custom providers
-   **Global hotkey** for instant voice capture
-   **Smart typing** directly into any app
-   **Menu bar integration** for quick access
-   **Auto-updates** with seamless restart
-   **Opt-in beta channel** for early preview builds

## Quick Start

[](#quick-start)

1.  Install with Homebrew:
    
    brew install --cask fluidvoice
    
    Or download the [latest release](https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice/releases/latest) and move it to Applications.
2.  Grant microphone and accessibility permissions when prompted
3.  Set your preferred hotkey in settings
4.  Optionally add an AI provider API key for enhanced transcription, keys are stored securely in your macOS Keychain. Make sure select "Always allow" for permissions
5.  Optional: opt in to beta builds in `Settings → Automatic Updates → Beta Releases`

## Requirements

[](#requirements)

-   macOS 15.0 (Sequoia) or later
-   Apple Silicon Mac (M1, M2, M3, M4)
-   Intel Macs are supported from 1.5.1 builds using Whisper models!
-   Microphone access
-   Accessibility permissions for typing

## Building from Source

[](#building-from-source)

git clone https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice.git
cd FluidVoice
open Fluid.xcodeproj

Build and run in Xcode. All dependencies are managed via Swift Package Manager.

## Contributing

[](#contributing)

Contributions are welcome! Please create an issue first to discuss any major changes or feature requests before submitting a pull request.

### Setting Up Your Development Environment

[](#setting-up-your-development-environment)

1.  **Clone the repository:**
    
    git clone https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice.git
    cd FluidVoice
    
2.  **Open in Xcode:**
    
    open Fluid.xcodeproj
    
3.  **Run from Xcode (one-time signing setup):**
    
    -   Target: `FluidVoice` → `Signing & Capabilities`
    -   Enable `Automatically manage signing`
    -   Pick your `Team` (Personal Team is fine)
    -   This is stored in `xcuserdata/` (gitignored), so it won’t affect your PR
4.  **Build and run** - All dependencies are managed via Swift Package Manager
    
5.  **Build only (no signing):**
    
    xcodebuild -project Fluid.xcodeproj -scheme Fluid -destination 'platform=macOS' build CODE\_SIGNING\_ALLOWED=NO
    
6.  **(Optional) Install pre-commit hook** to prevent accidental team ID commits:
    
    cp scripts/check-team-id.sh .git/hooks/pre-commit
    chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit
    

### Pull Request Guidelines

[](#pull-request-guidelines)

-   **Keep changes focused and atomic** - one feature or fix per PR
-   **Create an issue before raising a PR** so the work is easy to track and validate before review starts
-   **Discuss before raising a PR** for anything non-trivial so it is easier to merge and review tradeoffs up front
-   **Explain the pros and cons** of your approach when discussing larger changes
-   **Follow the PR template** when opening your pull request
-   **Update documentation** if adding new features
-   **Test thoroughly** on your machine before submitting
-   **Never commit personal team IDs or API keys** to `project.pbxproj`
-   **Check git diff** before committing to ensure no personal settings leaked in

## Connect

[](#connect)

Follow development updates on X: [@ALTIC\_DEV](https://x.com/ALTIC_DEV)

## Run integration dictation test

[](#run-integration-dictation-test)

xcodebuild test -project Fluid.xcodeproj -scheme Fluid -destination 'platform=macOS'

If you run into a test bundle load error related to code signing/Team ID, run without overriding code signing flags (the command above), or explicitly:

xcodebuild test -project Fluid.xcodeproj -scheme Fluid -destination 'platform=macOS' CODE\_SIGN\_STYLE=Automatic

CI uses unsigned builds:

xcodebuild test -project Fluid.xcodeproj -scheme Fluid -destination 'platform=macOS' CODE\_SIGNING\_REQUIRED=NO CODE\_SIGNING\_ALLOWED=NO

## Privacy & Analytics

[](#privacy--analytics)

FluidVoice is designed to be local-first, but it includes optional anonymous analytics, solely to keep track of FV usage and future feature development.

### What this helps with

[](#what-this-helps-with)

-   Feature usage patterns (for example dictation, command mode, and write mode)
-   Product reliability and performance tuning
-   Anonymous release health signals to prioritize fixes and improvements

### What is collected

[](#what-is-collected)

-   App version, build, and macOS version
-   Low-cardinality feature/config flags (for example app mode and major settings)
-   Approximate usage ranges (not exact values)
-   High-level success/error outcomes

### What is not collected

[](#what-is-not-collected)

-   Your voice, Raw audio or transcribed text
-   Selected text, prompts, or AI-generated responses
-   Terminal commands or outputs
-   Window titles, file names/paths, clipboard content, or typed content
-   or ANYTHING personal or private information.

### How to disable

[](#how-to-disable)

Analytics are enabled by default. You can disable or re-enable them at any time from:

`Settings → Share Anonymous Analytics`

### Why this exists

[](#why-this-exists)

Anonymous telemetry helps us understand what breaks, where performance can be improved, and which features matter most without collecting personal content. This helps us continue building what users want and if we should even continue developing features for you.

1: ---
2: name: jfdi-synthesis
3: description: Generate a weekly (or monthly) synthesis report from recent Obsidian session and memory files. Delegates to weekly-synthesis.sh if available; otherwise synthesizes inline and writes to ~/obsidian/Claude/Synthesis/weekly/.
4: ---
5: 
6: # JFDI Synthesis
7: 
8: Generate a synthesis report aggregating key decisions, learnings, patterns, and outcomes from recent sessions and memories.
9: 
10: ## Usage
11: 
12: ```
13: /jfdi-synthesis [--period weekly|monthly]
14: ```
15: 
16: - No argument: generate weekly synthesis (default)
17: - `--period monthly`: generate monthly rollup
18: 
19: ## What it does
20: 
21: 1. Calls `weekly-synthesis.sh` if it exists (preferred path)
22: 2. Falls back to inline synthesis: reads session and memory files from the last 7 days (or 30 for monthly), aggregates key themes, and writes a report to `~/obsidian/Claude/Synthesis/weekly/`
23: 
24: ## Instructions
25: 
26: ### Step 1: Check for synthesis script
27: 
28: ```bash
29: if [ -f ~/dotfiles/scripts/obsidian/weekly-synthesis.sh ]; then
30:   bash ~/dotfiles/scripts/obsidian/weekly-synthesis.sh 2>&1
31:   echo "SCRIPT_DONE"
32: fi
33: ```
34: 
35: If `SCRIPT_DONE` is printed, report the output and stop — the script handled everything.
36: 
37: ### Step 2: Inline fallback (script not found)
38: 
39: Determine the period:
40: - Weekly (default): last 7 days, ISO week format...

- Weekly rollups are then only as good as the session notes. They pull mostly `## Key Decisions` and `## Lessons` from session files at `scripts/obsidian/weekly-synthesis.sh:448-456` and `:577-583`.

**Why your example is thin**
- The note has:
  - branch name

  - no beads data
- That means the source context was already sparse.
- It also likely hit the fallback path, which means `claude --print` failed or returned empty at `scripts/obsidian/session-synthesize.sh:595-599`.

**How to make it reliably more detailed**
1. Make `.plan.md` the primary source of truth during work.

   - Keep `## Progress` current
   - Add `## Key Decisions`
   - Add `## Current State`

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # Opens the per-directory gwtt-prompt.local.md in nvim.
3: # Used by tmux bind E to resolve the correct prompt file for the active pane.
4: # Auto-creates per-repo file if it doesn't exist. No global fallback.
5: 
6: set -euo pipefail
7: 
8: # Get the pane's working directory (passed by tmux or default to CWD)
9: PANE_DIR="${1:-$(pwd)}"
10: 
11: # Find git root
12: GIT_ROOT=$(git -C "$PANE_DIR" rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || true)
13: 
14: if [[ -z "$GIT_ROOT" ]]; then
15:     echo "Error: not inside a git repository: $PANE_DIR"
16:     echo "Press enter to close..."
17:     read -r
18:     exit 1
19: fi
20: 
21: REPO_PROMPT="$GIT_ROOT/.claude/gwtt-prompt.local.md"
22: 
23: if [[ ! -f "$REPO_PROMPT" ]]; then
24:     mkdir -p "$GIT_ROOT/.claude"
25:     REPO_NAME=$(basename "$GIT_ROOT")
26:     printf '# %s — Task Prompt\n\nDescribe your task here.\n' "$REPO_NAME" >"$REPO_PROMPT"
27: fi
28: 
29: exec nvim -c 'setlocal textwidth=80 formatoptions+=t wrap linebreak' "$REPO_PROMPT"

(End of file - total 29 lines)
</content>

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: set -euo pipefail
3: 
4: # Probe the OpenAI API to detect whether the current account is rate-limited.
5: # Reads the OAuth access token from OpenCode's auth.json and makes a minimal
6: # chat completion request (gpt-4o-mini, max_tokens=1).
7: #
8: # Exit codes:
9: #   0 = available (API responded normally)
10: #   1 = rate-limited (usage limit reached)
11: #   2 = auth invalid or expired
12: #   3 = no auth file or no OpenAI entry
13: #
14: # Usage:
15: #   usage-check.sh              # human-readable output
16: #   usage-check.sh --quiet      # exit code only
17: #   usage-check.sh --json       # raw API response
18: #   usage-check.sh --token TOKEN # use a specific token instead of auth.json
19: 
20: AUTH_FILE="${OPENCODE_AUTH_FILE:-$HOME/.local/share/opencode/auth.json}"
21: QUIET=false
22: JSON_MODE=false
23: TOKEN_OVERRIDE=""
24: 
25: while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
26:     case "$1" in
27:     --quiet | -q)
28:         QUIET=true
29:         shift
30:         ;;
31:     --json | -j)
32:         JSON_MODE=true
33:         shift
34:         ;;
35:     --token)
36:         TOKEN_OVERRIDE="$2"
37:         shift 2
38:         ;;
39:     --auth-file)
40:         AUTH_FILE="$2"
41:         shift 2
42:         ;;
43:     *)
44:         shift
45:         ;;
46:     esac
47: done
48: 
49: log() {
50:     if ! $QUIET; then
51:         echo "$@"
52:     fi
53: }
54:

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: set -euo pipefail
3: 
4: # Probe the OpenAI API to detect whether the current account is rate-limited.
5: # Reads the OAuth access token from OpenCode's auth.json and makes a minimal
6: # chat completion request (gpt-4o-mini, max_tokens=1).
7: #
8: # Exit codes:
9: #   0 = available (API responded normally)
10: #   1 = rate-limited (usage limit reached)
11: #   2 = auth invalid or expired
12: #   3 = no auth file or no OpenAI entry
13: #
14: # Usage:
15: #   usage-check.sh              # human-readable output
16: #   usage-check.sh --quiet      # exit code only
17: #   usage-check.sh --json       # raw API response
18: #   usage-check.sh --token TOKEN # use a specific token instead of auth.json
19: 
20: AUTH_FILE="${OPENCODE_AUTH_FILE:-$HOME/.local/share/opencode/auth.json}"
21: QUIET=false
22: JSON_MODE=false
23: TOKEN_OVERRIDE=""
24: 
25: while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
26:     case "$1" in
27:     --quiet | -q)
28:         QUIET=true
29:         shift
30:         ;;
31:     --json | -j)
32:         JSON_MODE=true
33:         shift
34:         ;;
35:     --token)
36:         TOKEN_OVERRIDE="$2"
37:         shift 2
38:         ;;
39:     --auth-file)
40:         AUTH_FILE="$2"
41:         shift 2
42:         ;;
43:     *)
44:         shift
45:         ;;
46:     esac
47: done
48: 
49: log() {
50:     if ! $QUIET; then
51:         echo "$@"
52:     fi
53: }
54:

1: # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane
2: 
3: OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
4: 
5: ## Why the plugin is a material upgrade
6: 
7: - **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
8: - **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
9: - **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
10: - **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
11: - **Experi...

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # Launch OpenCode inside tmux with alternate-screen disabled so tmux copy-mode
3: # retains scrollback. Restores the prior setting when OpenCode exits.
4: set -euo pipefail
5: 
6: # Determine current window id so we can scope setw calls via tmux target
7: WINDOW=${TMUX_PANE:-}
8: 
9: restore_alternate_screen() {
10: 	if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
11: 		tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
12: 	else
13: 		tmux setw -w alternate-screen on >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
14: 	fi
15: }
16: 
17: trap restore_alternate_screen EXIT
18: 
19: if [ -n "$WINDOW" ]; then
20: 	tmux setw -t "$WINDOW" alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
21: else
22: 	tmux setw -w alternate-screen off >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
23: fi
24: 
25: exec opencode

(End of file - total 25 lines)

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: #
3: # claude-usage.sh - Check Claude Code usage via OAuth API
4: #
5: # Queries the undocumented OAuth usage endpoint to get current utilization
6: # and reset times for 5-hour and 7-day windows.
7: #
8: # Usage:
9: #   claude-usage.sh                  # Human-readable output
10: #   claude-usage.sh --json           # JSON output for scripting

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: set -euo pipefail
3: 
4: # Probe the OpenAI API to detect whether the current account is rate-limited.
5: # Reads the OAuth access token from OpenCode's auth.json and makes a minimal
6: # chat completion request (gpt-4o-mini, max_tokens=1).
7: #
8: # Exit codes:
9: #   0 = available (API responded normally)
10: #   1 = rate-limited (usage limit reached)
11: #   2 = auth invalid or expired
12: #   3 = no auth file or no OpenAI entry
13: #
14: # Usage:
15: #   usage-check.sh              # human-readable output
16: #   usage-check.sh --quiet      # exit code only
17: #   usage-check.sh --json       # raw API response
18: #   usage-check.sh --token TOKEN # use a specific token instead of auth.json
19: 
20: AUTH_FILE="${OPENCODE_AUTH_FILE:-$HOME/.local/share/opencode/auth.json}"
21: QUIET=false
22: JSON_MODE=false
23: TOKEN_OVERRIDE=""
24: 
25: while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
26:     case "$1" in
27:     --quiet | -q)
28:         QUIET=true
29:         shift
30:         ;;
31:     --json | -j)
32:         JSON_MODE=true
33:         shift
34:         ;;
35:     --token)
36:         TOKEN_OVERRIDE="$2"
37:         shift 2
38:         ;;
39:     --auth-file)
40:         AUTH_FILE="$2"
41:         shift 2
42:         ;;
43:     *)
44:         shift
45:         ;;
46:     esac
47: done
48: 
49: log() {
50:     if ! $QUIET; then
51:         echo "$@"
52:     fi
53: }
54:

2: #
3: # claude-usage.sh - Check Claude Code usage via OAuth API
4: #
5: # Queries the undocumented OAuth usage endpoint to get current utilization
6: # and reset times for 5-hour and 7-day windows.
7: #
8: # Usage:
9: #   claude-usage.sh                  # Human-readable output
10: #   claude-usage.sh --json           # JSON output for scripting
11: #   claude-usage.sh --available      # Exit 0 if capacity available, 1 if not
12: #   claude-usage.sh --wait           # Block until capacity is available
13: #   claude-usage.sh --threshold 80   # Custom threshold (default: 90%)
14: #   claude-usage.sh --config-dir ~/.claude-personal  # Use specific profile
15: #
16: # Exit codes:
17: #   0 = Success (or capacity available in --available mode)
18: #   1 = Rate limited / no capacity (in --available mode)
19: #   2 = Cannot get credentials
20: #   3 = API error
21: 
22: set -euo pipefail
23: 
24: SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

28: THRESHOLD=90

39: NC='\033[0m'

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: #
3: # weekly-synthesis.sh - Generate weekly synthesis of Claude Code sessions into Obsidian
4: #
5: # Scans synthesized session files (matching *-synth-*.md) from the last 7 days,
6: # aggregates structured data (projects, tickets, decisions, lessons), then calls
7: # claude --print to produce natural-language prose. Falls back to a structured
8: # template when claude is unavailable or fails.
9: #
10: # Idempotent: if this week's output file already exists and has valid frontmatter,
11: # exits cleanly unless --force is given. Atomic writes via mktemp + mv.
12: #
13: # Usage:
14: #   weekly-synthesis.sh [OPTIONS]
15: #
16: # Options:
17: #   --force         Regenerate even if this week's file already exists
18: #   --week YYYY-Www Override the ISO week (default: current week via date +%G-W%V)
19: #   --verbose       Show aggregated data before synthesis
20: #   --dry-run       Print what would be done without writing
21: #   --help          Show this help
22: 
23: set -euo pipefail
24: 
25: # --- Configuration ---
26: OBSIDIAN_VAULT="${OBSIDIAN_VAULT:-$HOME/obsidian}"
27: SESSIONS_DIR="$OBSIDIAN_VAULT/Claude/Sessions"
28: WEEKLY_DIR="$OBSIDIAN_VAULT/Claude/Synthesis/weekly"
29: MAX_PROMPT_BYTES=40000
30: SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
31: SESSION_SYNTHESIZER="$SCRIPT_DIR/session-synthesize.sh"
32:

2: name: jfdi-extract
3: description: Extract memories from Claude Code sessions using AI analysis. Batch mode processes up to 10 unextracted sessions; single-session mode targets a specific session by ID.
4: ---
5: 
6: # JFDI Extract
7: 
8: Extract memories from Claude Code session transcripts and write them to the Obsidian vault under `~/obsidian/Claude/Memories/`.
9: 
10: ## Usage
11: 
12: ```
13: /jfdi-extract [SESSION_ID]
14: ```
15: 
16: - No argument: batch mode — processes up to 10 unextracted sessions
17: - With SESSION_ID: single-session mode — processes only that session
18: 
19: ## Memory Types Extracted
20: 
21: - **decision**: Explicit implementation choices
22: - **insight**: New understanding or realizations
23: - **learning**: Technical knowledge gained
24: - **pattern**: Repeated behaviors detected
25: - **workflow**: Process improvements
26: - **commitment**: Commitments made during a session
27: 
28: ## Instructions
29: 
30: ### Batch Mode (default — no argument)
31: 
32: Check if the batch distillation script exists and run it:
33: 
34: ```bash
35: if [ -f ~/dotfiles/scripts/obsidian/session-distill-batch.sh ]; then
36:   bash ~/dotfiles/scripts/obsidian/session-distill-batch.sh --limit 10 --priority 2>&1
37: else
38:   echo "session-distill-batch.sh not found — falling back to manual guidance"
39: fi
40: ```

1: #!/bin/bash
2: 
3: # tmux URL handler script - opens URLs from tmux pane content
4: # Based on: https://dev.to/tomoviktor/best-way-to-open-urls-in-your-terminal-via-tmux-595b
5: # Smart capture: tries visible pane first, falls back to full scrollback if no URLs found
6: 
7: set -e
8: 
9: name="tmux-url-handler"
10: buffer_file_name="tmux-url-buffer-$$"
11: temp_file="/tmp/$buffer_file_name"
12: 
13: # Cleanup function
14: cleanup() {
15:     rm -f "$temp_file"
16: }
17: trap cleanup EXIT
18: 
19: # Function to capture URLs from pane content
20: # Args: $1 = capture mode ("visible" or "full")
21: capture_urls() {
22:     local capture_mode="$1"
23: 
24:     if [ "$capture_mode" = "full" ]; then
25:         tmux capture-pane -J -S - -p >"$temp_file"
26:     else
27:         tmux capture-pane -J -p >"$temp_file"
28:     fi
29: 
30:     # Extract scheme-qualified URLs and bare domains with common TLDs
31:     # Bare domains (e.g. google.com, bbc.co.uk) are normalized to https:// when opened
32:     grep -Eoi "(https?://[a-zA-Z0-9./?=_:%+#&~@!*(),;_%-]+|[a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9.-]*\.(com|org|net|io|dev|co\.uk|gov\.uk|edu|info|me|app)\b[/a-zA-Z0-9./?=_:%+#&~@!*(),;_%-]*)" "$temp_file" | sort | uniq || true
33: }
34: 
35: # Smart capture: try visible pane first, fall back to full scrollback if no URLs found
36: urls=$(capture_urls "visible")
37: 
38: if [ -z "$urls" ]; then
39:   ...

1: # opencode.nvim vs tmux Side Pane
2: 
3: OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
4: 
5: ## Why the plugin is a material upgrade
6: 
7: - **Zero-copy context** – prompts can reference placeholders such as `@this`, `@buffer`, `@diagnostics`, `@diff`, or `@visible`. The plugin resolves those against your current buffer/selection before sending them to OpenCode, so you stop yanking snippets into the side pane.
8: - **Prompt + operator ergonomics** – `require("opencode").ask`, `select`, and the operator wrapper let you reuse visual selections, ranges, and dot-repeat. Side panes are strictly linear terminals with no operator integration.
9: - **Editor-driven review loop** – when OpenCode proposes edits, the plugin opens a tab with `:diffpatch`, lets you accept/reject per hunk (`dp`, `do`, `]c`, `[c`), and reloads buffers on approval. The side pane requires manual patch application or copy/paste.
10: - **Permission + event awareness** – server-sent events surface inside Neovim via the `OpencodeEvent` autocmd, so you can hook custom automation (notifications, statusline, etc.). The pane view only shows whatever the TUI prints.
11: - **Experi...

-OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane where we manually copy/paste context. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin keeps the same backend assistant but embeds it directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
+OpenCode already lives in our tmux workflow: prefix + `Ctrl-s` + `O` opens the TUI in a dedicated pane. The terminal binary is `ocv` from [`leohenon/opencode-vim`](https://github.com/leohenon/opencode-vim), exposed through the repo-managed `scripts/bin/opencode` shim so existing launchers keep working while the TUI gains Vim motions, copy mode, and clipboard-register support. The [`opencode.nvim`](https://github.com/nickjvandyke/opencode.nvim) plugin is the complementary Neovim integration layer: it keeps the same backend assistant but embeds editor context directly in Neovim, which buys us tighter editor awareness, faster iteration, and better review UX.
@@
-- The backend assistant is still OpenCode, so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
+- The backend assistant is still OpenCode-compatible (`opencode` resolves to `ocv`), so authentication, model routing, hooks, and all `.opencode/` scripts remain unchanged.
@@
 5. Continue to use the tmux pane when you need a dedicated transcript view or when Neovim is not open—both entry points can coexist because they talk to the same OpenCode backend.
+
+## opencode-vim and opencode.nvim togeth...

parts.text:
**Exploring homebrew cask details**

I’m thinking about fetching the raw formula for a homebrew cask, like FluidVoice. It seems like I might not need to, but inspecting the official cask could be beneficial. I should use web fetching to gather that information. I want to read through the setup function around brew reconcile and casks as well. This should help clarify how everything works together!

FluidVoice is distributed as a Homebrew cask (`fluidvoice`), so this should fit the existing dotfiles package/bootstrap model rather than cloning/building from source. I’m checking where GUI casks and post-install setup notes belong.

403: # ============================================================================
404: # Brewfile Reconciliation
405: # ============================================================================
406: 
407: brewfile_mas_skip_ids() {
408:     local brewfile=$1
409:     local line
410:     local skip_ids=""
411: 
412:     [[ -f "$brewfile" ]] || return 0
413: 
414:     while IFS= read -r line; do
415:         [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*mas[[:space:]] ]] || continue
416: 
417:         if [[ "$line" =~ id:[[:space:]]*([0-9]+) ]]; then
418:             if [[ -z "$skip_ids" ]]; then
419:                 skip_ids="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
420:             else
421:                 skip_ids="$skip_ids ${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
422:             fi
423:         fi
424:     done <"$brewfile"
425: 
426:     printf '%s' "$skip_ids"
427: }
428: 
429: app_store_signed_in() {
430:     command_exists mas && mas account >/dev/null 2>&1
431: }
432: 
433: run_brew_bundle() {
434:     local brewfile=$1
435:     local mas_skip_ids=${2:-}
436: 
437:     if [[ -n "$mas_skip_ids" ]]; then
438:         HOMEBREW_BUNDLE_MAS_SKIP="$mas_skip_ids" brew bundle --file="$brewfile" 2>&1 ||
439:             log_verbose "Brewfile reconciliation completed with warnings"
440:     else
441:         brew bundle --file="$brewfile" 2>&1 ||
442:             log_verbose "Brewfile reconciliation completed with warnings"
443:     fi
444: }
445: 
446: prompt_app_store_sign_in() {
447:     if [[ "$NO_CONFIRM" == "true" ]]; then
448:         print_warning "Mac App Store is not signed in; --no-confirm set, skipping Mac App Store apps"
449:         return 1
450:     fi
451: 
452:     if [[ ! -t 0 ]]; then
453:         print_warning "Mac App Store is not signed in and setup is non-interactive; skipping Mac App Store apps"
454:         return 1
455:     fi
456: 
457:     local answer
458:     read -r -p "$(echo -e "${YELLOW}Mac App Store is not signed in. Open App Store now to sign in before installing Mac App Store apps? [y/N]${NC} ")" answer
459: 
460:     case "$answer" in
461:     [Yy]*)
462:         open -a "App Store" 2>/dev/null || print_warning "Could not open App Store automatically"
463:         read -r -p "$(echo -e "${YELLOW}Sign into the App Store, then press Enter to continue (or type s to skip): ${NC}")" answer
464: 
465:         case "$answer" in
466:         [Ss]*)
467:             print_warning "Skipping Mac App Store apps"
468:             return 1
469:             ;;
470:         esac
471: 
472:         if app_store_signed_in; then
473:             print_success "Mac App Store sign-in detected"
474:             return 0
475:         fi
476: 
477:         print_warning "Mac App Store sign-in was not detected; skipping Mac App Store apps"
478:         return 1
479:         ;;
480:     *)
481:         print_warning "Skipping Mac App Store apps"
482:         return 1
483:         ;;
484:     esac
485: }
486: 
487: reconcile_brewfile() {
488:     local brewfile=$1
489:     local mas_skip_ids
490:     local reconciled_without_mas=false
491: 
492:     mas_skip_ids=$(brewfile_mas_skip_ids "$brewfile")
493: 
494:     if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
495:         print_warning "DRY RUN: Would run brew bundle --file=$brewfile"
496:         if [[ -n "$mas_skip_ids" ]]; then
497:             print_warning "DRY RUN: Would verify Mac App Store sign-in before installing MAS apps"
498:         fi
499:         return 0
500:     fi
501: 
502:     if [[ -z "$mas_skip_ids" ]]; then
503:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile"
504:         return 0
505:     fi
506: 
507:     if [[ "${DETECTED_OS:-$(detect_os)}" != "macos" ]]; then
508:         print_warning "Skipping Mac App Store apps on non-macOS"
509:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile" "$mas_skip_ids"
510:         return 0
511:     fi
512: 
513:     if ! command_exists mas; then
514:         print_step "Installing Brewfile dependencies before Mac App Store apps..."
515:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile" "$mas_skip_ids"
516:         reconciled_without_mas=true
517:     fi
518: 
519:     if ! command_exists mas; then
520:         print_warning "mas CLI is unavailable; skipping Mac App Store apps"
521:         return 0
522:     fi
523: 
524:     if app_store_signed_in; then
525:         print_success "Mac App Store sign-in detected"
526:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile"
527:         return 0
528:     fi
529: 
530:     if prompt_app_store_sign_in; then
531:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile"
532:         return 0
533:     fi
534: 
535:     if [[ "$reconciled_without_mas" != "true" ]]; then
536:         run_brew_bundle "$brewfile" "$mas_skip_ids"
537:     fi
538: }
539: 
540: # ============================================================================
541: # Installation Phases
542: # ============================================================================
543: 
544: phase_1_core_packages() {
545:     [[ "$SKIP_PACKAGES" == "true" ]] && return 0
546:     [[ $(is_step_complete "core_packages") == "true" ]] && return 0
547: 
548:     print_header "Phase 1: Core Packages"
549: 
550:     pm_update
551:     pm_upgrade
552: 
553:     # Reconcile Brewfile — installs missing, upgrades outdated
554:     if command_exists brew && [[ -f "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile" ]]; then
555:         print_step "Reconciling Brewfile..."
556:         reconcile_brewfile "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile"
557:     fi
558: 
559:     install_packages_from_profile "$PROFILE" "core"
560: 
561:     # Enable Homebrew background auto-updates (daily update + upgrade + cleanup)
562:     if command_exists brew && brew tap | grep -q "domt4/autoupdate" 2>/dev/null; then
563:         if [[ "$DRY_RUN" != "true" ]]; then
564:             print_step "Enabling Homebrew background auto-updates..."
565:             brew autoupdate start --upgrade --cleanup --immediate 2>/dev/null &&
566:                 print_success "Homebrew auto-update enabled (24h interval)" ||
567:                 log_verbose "Homebrew auto-update configuration skipped"
568:         else
569:             print_warning "DRY RUN: Would enable Homebrew background auto-updates"
570:         fi
571:     fi
572: 
573:     mark_step_complete "core_packages"
574: }
575: 
576: phase_2_cli_tools() {
577:     [[ "$SKIP_PACKAGES" == "true" ]] && return 0
578:     [[ $(is_step_complete "cli_tools") == "true" ]] && return 0
579: 
580:     print_header "Phase 2: CLI Tools"
581: 
582:     # Try package manager first
583:     install_packages_from_profile "$PROFILE" "cli_tools"
584: 
585:     # Install binaries for tools not in repos
586:     install_binaries_from_profile "$PROFILE"
587: 
588:     if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
589:         print_warning "DRY RUN: Skipping CLI tool post-install configuration"
590:         mark_step_complete "cli_tools"
591:         return 0
592:     fi
593: 
594:     # Configure bat with Catppuccin Mocha theme
595:     if command_exists bat; then
596:         print_step "Configuring bat with Catppuccin Mocha theme..."
597:         mkdir -p "$(bat --config-dir)/themes"

599:             -o "$(bat --config-dir)/themes/Catppuccin Mocha.tmTheme"; then

# Brewfile Reconciliation

brewfile_mas_skip_ids() {
    local brewfile=$1
    local line
    local skip_ids=""

    [[ -f "$brewfile" ]] || return 0

**Planning Brewfile setups**

I need to inspect profiles to determine if the Brewfile is sufficient. It's possible that during phase 1, the brew bundle will install everything in the Brewfile, but I should check if the setup test expects `setup.sh` to contain `"firefox"`. Adding cask to the Brewfile might not be enough to satisfy the test, so I should search specifically for Firefox in the test-filter Brewfile. There’s also a `setup.sh` where phase 7 installs GUI apps separately, so I need to look closely at that.

210:         if [ -f "$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/$script" ]; then
211:             run_test "$script syntax valid" "bash -n '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/$script'"
212:         fi
213:     done
214: }
215: 
216: test_brewfile() {
217:     echo -e "${BLUE}--- Brewfile Tests ---${NC}"
218:     run_test "Brewfile exists" "[ -f '$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile' ]"
219: 
220:     # Check for duplicate entries
221:     if [ -f "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile" ]; then
222:         DUPES=$(grep -E '^(brew|cask|mas) ' "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile" | sort | uniq -d | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
223:         run_test "No duplicate Brewfile entries" "[ '$DUPES' -eq 0 ]"
224:     fi
225: }
226: 
227: test_mcp() {
228:     echo -e "${BLUE}--- MCP Parity Tests ---${NC}"
229: 
230:     # Check Claude Desktop config exists
231:     local desktop_config="$DOTFILES_ROOT/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json"
232:     local mcp_config="$DOTFILES_ROOT/.mcp.json"
233:     run_test "Claude Desktop config exists" "[ -f '$desktop_config' ]"
234: 
235:     if [ -f "$desktop_config" ]; then
236:         run_test "Claude Desktop config is valid JSON" "python3 -c \"import json; json.load(open('$desktop_config'))\""
237:     fi
238: 
239:     # Check .mcp.json exists
240:     run_test ".mcp.json exists" "[ -f '$mcp_config' ]"
241:     if [ -f "$mcp_config" ]; then
242:         run_test ".mcp.json is valid JSON" "python3 -c \"import json; json.load(open('$mcp_config'))\""
243:     fi
244: 
245:     if [ -f "$desktop_config" ] && [ -f "$mcp_config" ]; then
246:         run_test "Desktop MCP matches .mcp.json" "python3 -c \"import json; repo=json.load(open('$mcp_config')).get('mcpServers', {}); desk=json.load(open('$desktop_config')).get('mcpServers', {}); assert desk == repo\""
247:         run_test ".mcp.json MCP servers use bunx" "python3 -c \"import json; servers=json.load(open('$mcp_config')).get('mcpServers', {}); assert servers and all(server.get('command') == 'bunx' for server in servers.values())\""
248:         run_test "setup.sh registers .mcp.json stdio servers" "python3 -c \"import json, pathlib, re; servers=set(json.load(open('$mcp_config')).get('mcpServers', {})); setup=pathlib.Path('$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/setup.sh').read_text(); configured=set(re.findall(r'claude mcp add --scope user ([a-z0-9-]+) ', setup)); assert servers <= configured\""
249:     fi
250: 
251:     run_test "setup.sh uses bunx for shared stdio MCP servers" "python3 -c \"import pathlib; text=pathlib.Path('$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/setup.sh').read_text(); required=['context7 bunx', 'steampipe bunx', 'playwright bunx', 'drawio bunx']; assert all(item in text for item in required)\""
252:     run_test "setup.sh keeps deepwiki SSE exception" "grep -q 'claude mcp add --scope user --transport sse deepwiki https://mcp.deepwiki.com/sse' '$DOTFILES_ROOT/scripts/setup.sh'"
253: 
254:     # deniedMcpServers: blocks unused claude.ai managed integrations to reclaim KV-cache tokens
255:     local settings="$DOTFILES_ROOT/.claude/settings.json"
256:     if [ -f "$settings" ]; then
257:         run_test "settings.json declares deniedMcpServers" "python3 -c \"import json; s=json.load(open('$settings')); assert isinstance(s.get('deniedMcpServers'), list) and len(s['deniedMcpServers']) > 0\""
258:         run_test "deniedMcpServers includes unused integrations" "python3 -c \"import json; denied=set(json.load(open('$settings')).get('deniedMcpServers', [])); required={'claude_ai_Notion','claude_ai_Linear','claude_ai_Invideo'}; assert required <= denied, f'missing: {required - denied}'\""
259:         run_test "deniedMcpServers keeps used integrations unblocked" "python3 -c \"import json; denied=set(json.load(open('$settings')).get('deniedMcpServers', [])); used={'claude_ai_Atlassian','claude_ai_Gmail','claude_ai_Google_Calendar','claude_ai_Google_Drive'}; assert not (used & denied), f'blocks used: {used & denied}'\""
260:     fi
261: }
262:

test_brewfile() {
    echo -e "${BLUE}--- Brewfile Tests ---${NC}"
    run_test "Brewfile exists" "[ -f '$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile' ]"

    # Check for duplicate entries
    if [ -f "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile" ]; then
        DUPES=$(grep -E '^(brew|cask|mas) ' "$DOTFILES_ROOT/homebrew/Brewfile" | sort | uniq -d | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
        run_test "No duplicate Brewfile entries" "[ '$DUPES' -eq 0 ]"
    fi
}

content.summary:
User request, exact quote: "Add this to my dotfiles https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice and set it up so that we can use it". A system reminder says Plan Mode is active, so this phase is strictly read-only: no file edits, modifications, install commands, commits, or other state changes. Only observe, analyze, and plan.

Investigation performed without edits:

FluidVoice upstream details:
- Repository: `https://github.com/altic-dev/FluidVoice` (`altic-dev/FluidVoice`).
- README describes FluidVoice as a fully open-source local-first macOS voice-to-text dictation app with AI enhancement.
- Official install method: `brew install --cask fluidvoice`; manual download from latest GitHub release is also supported.
- Latest release shown in GitHub page: `FluidVoice v1.5.13`, May 2, 2026.
- Homebrew Formulae page confirms cask `fluidvoice`, current version `1.5.13`, app name `FluidVoice`, description “Offline voice-to-text dictation app with AI enhancement”, homepage `https://altic.dev/fluid`, cask API `/api/cask/fluidvoice.json`, cask code in Homebrew/homebrew-cask, requirements `macOS >= 15`.
- README requirements: macOS 15.0 Sequoia or later; Apple Silicon Mac preferred; Intel Macs supported from 1.5.1 builds using Whisper models; microphone access; accessibility permissions for typing.
- Feature set: real-time transcription, global hotkey, smart typing into any app, menu bar integration, auto-updates, live preview overlay, command mode, write mode, history/stats.
- Supported engines/models: Parakeet Flash, Parakeet TDT v3/v2, Cohere Transcribe, Apple Speech, Whisper. Parakeet/Cohere are Apple Silicon-oriented; Whisper supports Intel and broad language coverage.
- Optional AI provider API keys can be added for enhanced transcription (OpenAI, Groq, custom providers); keys are stored in macOS Keychain according to README. This should not be encoded in dotfiles.
- Permissions are runtime/user action: grant Microphone and Accessibility prompts; optional beta releases in Settings; optional analytics disable via Settings -> Share Anonymous Analytics. These cannot be fully automated safely via dotfiles and should be documented/printed as post-install guidance.

Dotfiles repository constraints and relevant structure:
- Working directory/repo: `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles`.
- Repo instructions require new CLI tools in both `homebrew/Brewfile` and `scripts/setup.sh`, but FluidVoice is a GUI Homebrew cask, not a CLI. GUI apps live in Brewfile and `phase_9_fonts_and_apps` in setup if explicit install/verification is wanted.
- `homebrew/Brewfile` currently has taps at lines 1-39, packages and casks. Relevant current GUI/cask area:
  - `cask "1password-cli"` at line 135 under AI Tools.
  - `cask "ollama-app"` at line 149 under Self-Hosted LLM Tools.
  - GUI Applications section at lines 301-305 contains `cask "firefox"`, `cask "nikitabobko/tap/aerospace"`, `cask "wezterm"`, `cask "session-manager-plugin"`.
  - Mac App Store section line 308 has `mas "iWallpaper - Live Wallpaper", id: 1552826194`.
- Best Brewfile insertion point for FluidVoice is the GUI Applications section around lines 301-305, probably after `wezterm` or before `session-manager-plugin`, as `cask "fluidvoice"` with a short comment if desired.

Setup script findings:
- `scripts/setup.sh` is the main machine bootstrap with phases and Brewfile reconciliation.
- Brewfile reconciliation is in `scripts/setup.sh:403-538`:
  - `run_brew_bundle()` calls `brew bundle --file="$brewfile"`.
  - `reconcile_brewfile()` handles Mac App Store sign-in and MAS skipping.
- `phase_1_core_packages` at `scripts/setup.sh:544-574` calls `pm_update`, `pm_upgrade`, then reconciles the entire Brewfile if Homebrew exists (`scripts/setup.sh:553-557`). This means adding `cask "fluidvoice"` to Brewfile is already enough for normal bootstrap installation via `brew bundle`.
- There is also a dedicated GUI phase: `phase_9_fonts_and_apps()` starts at `scripts/setup.sh:1889`; grep found relevant lines:
  - line 1893: print header `Phase 9: Fonts & Applications`.
  - lines 1913 and 1926: font cask list/install.
  - lines 1943, 1945, 1946: app list includes `firefox`, `wezterm`, `nikitabobko/tap/aerospace`.
  - line 1961: `brew install --cask "${apps_to_install[@]}"`.
  - lines 1967-1972 onward: AeroSpace CLI symlink/post-install check.
  - lines 2012-2014: invokes `scripts/setup/firefox-setup.sh`.
  - line 2838-2839: main flow invokes `phase_9_fonts_and_apps`.
- Because `phase_9_fonts_and_apps` has an explicit app array, adding `fluidvoice` there would satisfy the repo rule “setup.sh installs GUI app” and gives clearer direct install behavior when phase 9 runs or when Brewfile reconciliation is skipped/partial.
- The grep output also showed `scripts/test-filter.sh` contains a browser test asserting setup installs Firefox by looking for `"firefox"` in setup. No existing FluidVoice test exists.

Profiles and test context:
- Profile files found: `scripts/profiles/minimal.conf`, `standard.conf`, `dev.conf`, `ops.conf`, `comprehensive.conf`.
- `scripts/profiles/standard.conf` and `minimal.conf` contain tool sections but no GUI app section. Standard has `[cli_tools]` with AI/Local LLM `ollama=true`, `gemini-cli=true`; development etc. No app/cask toggles. Comprehensive similarly has package toggles, no GUI apps section.
- Setup’s phase 9 uses hardcoded app/font arrays rather than profile-driven GUI app toggles, so adding FluidVoice to the phase 9 `apps_to_install` array is the minimal setup.sh change.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh:216-225` has `test_brewfile()` checking Brewfile exists and duplicate `brew|cask|mas` entries. Adding `cask "fluidvoice"` must avoid duplicates.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh:263-299` has browser automation tests including:
  - line 276: Brewfile installs Firefox by grepping `cask "firefox"`.
  - line 277: setup.sh installs Firefox GUI app by grepping `"firefox"`.
  - many Firefox-specific tests; these are unrelated.
- Potential minimal tests after implementation: `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile` for duplicate Brewfile entries; `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax` or `bash -n scripts/setup.sh`; if adding FluidVoice-specific smoke tests to `scripts/test-filter.sh` then run relevant group, but not necessary unless user wants tests for this integration.

Likely implementation plan once Plan Mode is exited/approved:
1. Add `cask "fluidvoice"` to `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/homebrew/Brewfile` under `# GUI Applications` with a concise comment like `# Offline macOS dictation app`.
2. Add `"fluidvoice"` to the `apps_to_install` array inside `scripts/setup.sh` `phase_9_fonts_and_apps()` near existing casks (`firefox`, `wezterm`, `nikitabobko/tap/aerospace`). This provides explicit setup support beyond Brewfile reconciliation and satisfies dotfiles rules to update setup.sh when adding new tools/apps.
3. Add post-install guidance in `scripts/setup.sh` after the app install step or near phase 9 app handling, gated on macOS and installed app presence, to print non-automatable setup steps: open FluidVoice once, grant Microphone and Accessibility permissions, set global hotkey, optionally choose model/provider, optionally disable analytics. Avoid trying to automate TCC privacy grants or storing API keys.
4. Optionally add a small Fish wrapper/function only if useful. Because FluidVoice is a GUI app, likely no Fish function is necessary. If adding one, it should be `.config/fish/functions/fluidvoice.fish` and simply `open -a FluidVoice` or show install/help; but minimal approach is no new function unless user wants CLI convenience.
5. Validate with read/syntax tests: `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`, `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`, and perhaps `scripts/setup.sh --dry-run --skip-dotfiles --skip-shells` or targeted dry-run if appropriate. In plan mode no commands were run beyond reads/fetches.

Open decision before implementation:
- Whether to include a `fluidvoice` Fish helper alias/function (`fluidvoice` -> `open -a FluidVoice`) or keep this as pure Homebrew cask + setup guidance. Minimal recommended approach: no wrapper; launch via Spotlight/menu bar or `open -a FluidVoice`.

parts.text: ▣ DCP | -95.1K removed, +2.2K summary — Compression #1

Plan to add FluidVoice safely:

1. Add Homebrew cask
   - Edit `homebrew/Brewfile`.
   - Add `cask "fluidvoice"` under `# GUI Applications`.
   - This lets normal `brew bundle` install it.

2. Wire setup support
   - Edit `scripts/setup.sh`.
   - Add `"fluidvoice"` to the `apps_to_install` array in `phase_9_fonts_and_apps()`.
   - Add a short post-install note for required manual permissions:
     - Open FluidVoice once.
     - Grant Microphone access.
     - Grant Accessibility access so it can type into apps.
     - Configure global hotkey/model/provider in FluidVoice settings.
     - Do not store API keys in dotfiles; use the app/Keychain.

3. Avoid unnecessary wrapper
   - I recommend not adding a Fish function unless you specifically want `fluidvoice` as a terminal launcher.
   - Since this is a menu bar GUI app, Spotlight/Launchpad or `open -a FluidVoice` is enough.

4. Validate
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`.
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`.
   - Optionally run setup dry-run if the script supports the relevant path cleanly.

I’m ready to implement this once Plan Mode is lifted.
Implement this
I’ll apply the minimal integration: add the cask to the Brewfile, include it in setup’s GUI app phase, then run targeted validation.

# Scripts Index

112 scripts and 20 subdirectories supporting the dotfiles ecosystem. Scripts use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` with `set -euo pipefail`.

## Key Entry Points

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `setup.sh` | Main machine bootstrap (Homebrew, stow, tools, configs) |
| `setup-mobile-coding.sh` | Mosh + Tailscale for remote access |
| `setup-selfhost-llm.sh` | Ollama + Open WebUI local LLM |

## Subdirectories

### Core Infrastructure
| Directory | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `setup/` | macOS defaults, system preferences |
| `harness/` | Drift detection, diagnostics for dotfiles health |
| `lib/` | Shared bash libraries and utilities |
| `bin/` | Standalone executables added to PATH |
| `hooks/` | Git hooks and CI integration |
| `ci-hooks/` | CI-specific hook scripts |
| `tests/` | Shell script tests |
| `profiles/` | Shell profile management |

### Agent & AI
| Directory | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `claude/` | Claude Code helper scripts |
| `codex/` | Codex CLI integration and account management |
| `otel/` | OpenTelemetry LGTM stack (docker-compose, Grafana dashboards) |
| `obsidian/` | Obsidian vault management scripts |
| `openclaw/` | OpenClaw AI platform config |

### DevOps & Containers
| Directory | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `docker/` | Dockerfile and container testing |
| `devcontainer/` | Devcontainer credential export, auto-login |
| `kubernetes/` | K8s helper scripts |
| `manifests/` | K8s manifest files (with README) |
| `linux/` | Linux-specific setup scripts |
| `windows/` | Windows/WSL setup |
| `windows-vm/` | Windows VM provisioning |

### Domain-Specific
| Directory | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `youtube/` | YouTube transcript fetcher (yt-transcript.py) |
| `anki/` | Anki flashcard generation |
| `cv/` | CV compilation and LaTeX tooling |
| `aws/` | AWS helper scripts |
| `tmux/` | tmux watcher, session management |
| `pihole/` | Pi-hole DNS management |
| `ticket-queue/` | Ticket queue management |
| `tools/` | Standalone tool scripts |

## Conventions

- Bash scripts: `set -euo pipefail`, quote all variables, snake_case functions
- File naming: kebab-case (e.g., `setup-mobile-coding.sh`)
- Shared code: import from `lib/` directory
- Never use `((var++))` with `set -e` — use `var=$((var + 1))` instead

2. Wire setup support
   - Edit `scripts/setup.sh`.
   - Add `"fluidvoice"` to the `apps_to_install` array in `phase_9_fonts_and_apps()`.
   - Add a short post-install note for required manual permissions:
     - Open FluidVoice once.
     - Grant Microphone access.
     - Grant Accessibility access so it can type into apps.
     - Configure global hotkey/model/provider in FluidVoice settings.
     - Do not store API keys in dotfiles; use the app/Keychain.

3. Avoid unnecessary wrapper
   - I recommend not adding a Fish function unless you specifically want `fluidvoice` as a terminal launcher.
   - Since this is a menu bar GUI app, Spotlight/Launchpad or `open -a FluidVoice` is enough.

4. Validate
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`.
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`.
   - Optionally run setup dry-run if the script supports the relevant path cleanly.

3. Avoid unnecessary wrapper
   - I recommend not adding a Fish function unless you specifically want `fluidvoice` as a terminal launcher.
   - Since this is a menu bar GUI app, Spotlight/Launchpad or `open -a FluidVoice` is enough.

4. Validate
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile`.
   - Run `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax`.
   - Optionally run setup dry-run if the script supports the relevant path cleanly.

**Clarifying testing steps**

**Providing accessibility steps**

To enable FluidVoice, go to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Accessibility. In FluidVoice, select the local engine, like Apple Speech or Whisper, without needing a provider key. 

Then, open TextEdit or Notes, use the hotkey or menu to start recording, say a sentence, and check if the text appears. If nothing is typed, please check your Accessibility settings and microphone permissions.

If the app doesn’t open due to Gatekeeper, navigate to System Settings > Privacy & Security > Open Anyway. Just a reminder, while Plan Mode is active, I can't launch it for you.

**Outlining testing steps**

I need to comply with "Fish primary," and I know that `open -a` works in Fish shell without using `&&`.

I'll keep everything on a single level and avoid nested bullets, so it remains straightforward. I should mention that since Plan Mode is active, I can't run commands directly from here. Test run:

1. Launch FluidVoice:
   `open -a FluidVoice`

2. Grant permissions when prompted:
   Microphone access and Accessibility access.

3. If Accessibility prompt does not appear:
   Open `System Settings > Privacy & Security > Accessibility`, then enable `FluidVoice`.

4. Open a text field:
   Try Notes, TextEdit, Safari, or this chat box.

5. Start dictation from FluidVoice:
   Use its menu bar icon or configured hotkey, speak a short sentence, and confirm it types into the active app.

6. For fully local/free testing:
   Choose a local model/engine in FluidVoice settings and skip any provider/API key setup.
